summary: Kenneth discusses the risks of trading into earnings weeks, using his portfolio as a case study. He demonstrates how major earnings announcements (HIMS, D-Wave, etc.) created significant volatility that hurt several positions, and emphasizes the importance of considering whether to sit out high-volatility earnings weeks rather than blindly entering trades.
takeaways: Avoid or reduce trading activity during major earnings weeks when multiple big-name companies report simultaneously
Recognize that covered calls can trap you in losing positions—Kenneth has $23,423 stuck in HIMS at $58 cost basis (now $45-46) and cannot sell calls profitably
When stock drops significantly below your cost basis, covered call premium becomes too small to justify the risk
Monitor earnings dates on your positions (HIMS Monday, D-Wave Thursday, Oscar Health, etc.) to anticipate volatility spikes
Consider waiting until mid-week (Tuesday/Wednesday) after earnings volatility settles before selling new covered calls for better premiums
Earnings can cause sharp directional moves (ELF: $131 to $122, HIMS: $58 to $45-46) that wipe out premium gains
Accept opportunity cost: liquidity from car sale ($12k) should have stayed in cash or deployed cautiously rather than chasing ELF on an earnings-volatile week

strategy_candidates.change: Implement a 'no-new-entry' rule on Mondays when major earnings are scheduled; preserve dry powder for mid-week entries post-earnings volatility
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth notes that Monday is 'going to be the big earnings week' with multiple companies reporting, then admits he'll make smaller trades Monday and only deployed $18.2k in new capital despite having $55.3k total deployed. Suggests ex-post that sitting out would have been prudent.

strategy_candidates.change: Set a minimum premium threshold based on delta/moneyness rather than absolute dollar amounts; skip covered call sales on deep OTM positions like Oscar Health ($18 stock, $21.50 strike) where premium is 'going to be very small'
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth explicitly states he may wait until Tuesday/Wednesday when Oscar Health stock recovers so he can 'sell a covered call for' better premium, indicating earnings-driven downside traps low-premium positions that waste capital allocation.

title: Part 21: 10/27-10/31 - risks of trading into earnings
